parentQuestion: A marathon runner develops severe knee pain. MRI shows a torn meniscus (fibrocartilage). The orthopedic surgeon explains this will heal very slowly and may require surgical repair. What property of this tissue makes healing so difficult?

scaffolds.q: Healing requires delivery of nutrients, oxygen, and repair cells to the injury.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Without these supplies, tissues can't repair themselves effectively.

scaffolds.q: Blood vessels deliver healing supplies directly to injured tissues.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Good blood supply = fast healing. Blood brings inflammatory cells, growth factors, and nutrients.
Yes! 'A-vascular' means WITHOUT blood vessels. The 'a-' prefix means 'not' or 'without.'

scaffolds.q: Cartilage is AVASCULAR - it has NO blood vessels.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! This includes the meniscus (fibrocartilage). Nutrients reach it only by slow diffusion.

scaffolds.q: Without blood vessels, healing supplies must DIFFUSE in slowly from the edges.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! This is much slower than direct blood delivery. Days instead of hours.

scaffolds.q: Avascular tissues heal SLOWLY or sometimes not at all.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! That's why cartilage injuries are so problematic - minimal blood supply means minimal healing.

scaffolds.q: The meniscus often requires surgery because it won't heal well on its own.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Surgeons often remove damaged portions because waiting for healing may be futile.

scaffolds.q: The meniscus heals slowly because fibrocartilage is AVASCULAR (no blood supply).
scaffolds.explain: Correct! No blood vessels means slow nutrient delivery and poor access for repair cells.

scaffolds.q: The answer is AVASCULAR (no blood supply) - cartilage can't heal quickly without blood vessels.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! The tissue has cells, growth factors can reach it eventually, but the lack of blood supply is the critical limitation.
